text: The robotic surgical system is docked, revealing a setup with a visibly redundant sigmoid colon in the patient's abdominal cavity.
The surgeon attempts to retract the redundant sigmoid colon, demonstrating the challenge in pulling it back and securing it proximally during the rectopexy procedure.
The robotic surgical arm on the left is visibly struggling with the positioning, showing significant angulation from this camera angle.
During the laparoscopic ventral rectopexy, the surgeon uses an endo loop to encircle an appendix epiploica on the colon, preparing to release it at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon secures the sigmoid colon using a strap through an appendix epiploica, visibly anchoring it in place during the laparoscopic ventral rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to secure the sigmoid colon, demonstrating the suturing technique on the visible colon tissue.
The surgeon demonstrates how the procedure straightens the redundant sigmoid colon, visibly aligning it within the surgical field.
The biologic graft, placed three years earlier, is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the robust appearance of the biologic graft within the surgical field, noting its integration with the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon pulls on the biologic graft, visibly straightening the redundant sigmoid colon.

The surgeon observes the thickened and white peritoneum in the pouch of Douglas, resembling a hernia sac. Despite previous surgery, the pelvis appears as if no operation was performed, except for the presence of a previous biologic graft. The surgeon plans to fix the mesh on the anterior wall of the rectum as high as possible.
The ureter is clearly visible on the right side of the surgical field.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum medial to the previously placed mesh, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon excises the redundant peritoneum in the pouch of Douglas, carefully removing the thickened, white tissue resembling a hernia sac.
The surgeon inserts an instrument into the uterus from the vaginal side, lifting it anteriorly to facilitate exposure and manipulation during the rectopexy procedure.
An assistant places a rectal sizer in the rectum to aid in identifying the rectal wall during the procedure.
The surgeon initially uses a hook instrument for laparoscopic manipulation but switches to scissors for greater versatility in tissue handling during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon attempts to use robotic scissors but finds them ineffective for cutting, opting instead for laparoscopic scissors to perform precise incisions.
The surgeon performs dissection, revealing a distinct layer of fat beneath the thickened peritoneum, even in a thin patient.

The surgeon attempts to pull and straighten the rectum, highlighting the challenge of maintaining orientation with the rectal wall. Rectal sizes are used to aid in identifying the correct anatomical structure.
The pneumoperitoneum causes the pelvic floor to balloon outward, requiring the surgeon to apply traction to counteract this effect while positioning the sigmoid colon with assistance.
The surgeon excises a redundant patch of Douglas tissue, carefully removing it from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon periodically reorients the view to ensure accurate identification of the rectal wall during the procedure.
The surgeon assesses the surgical field, noting the presence of redundant peritoneum that remains to be excised.
The surgeon re-peritonealizes the biologic graft, positioning it beneath the peritoneum to enhance collagen deposition and scar tissue strength.
The surgeon finalizes the dissection of the pouch of Douglas, ensuring the area is adequately prepared for the next steps in the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon proceeds with proximal dissection, moving upwards along the rectum to further expose and prepare the area for rectopexy.
The surgeon performs a posterior rectopexy, securing the sigmoid colon in place, as part of the procedure to address rectal prolapse.
The surgeon performs an initial dissection to expose the presacral tissue, preparing the area for mesh fixation.
The surgeon navigates within the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane posteriorly, focusing on the anatomical structures relevant to the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon performs dissection in the lateral region, visibly affecting some of the lateral nerves during the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to perform a suture rectopexy using a proline suture, addressing the recurrent case by securing the rectum to the presacral tissue.
The presacral tissue is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon displays the proline suture, preparing to use it for the suture rectopexy procedure.
The robotic surgical system is used to take precise bites of tissue during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon embeds the suture into the tissue of the mid rectum, securing the proximal graft with a robust fixation method.
The surgeon uses a Prolene suture, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a stat tack on the appendix every block of the sigmoid colon, visibly straightening the colon further.
The surgeon presents the graft, which is prepared for use in the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon takes full-thickness bites through the rectal wall, likely using sutures to secure the tissue.
The surgeon places final sutures on the anterior wall, securing the tissue in place.
The surgeon displays a graft with a broader middle section, measuring three centimeters in width, and having a relatively rectangular shape.
The surgeon approaches the mid rectum, preparing to perform surgical actions in this area.
The mid rectum is rolled over the front of the mesh, demonstrating the positioning of the rectum in relation to the mesh during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon clears presacral tissue in a patient with more adipose tissue to ensure solid suture bites during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon maneuvers the suture around the rectal tissue, ensuring proper placement and tension during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon places three pedia sutures in the proximal graft, securing it to the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon ensures that the sutures on the proximal side are secured with solid bites, focusing on anchoring the graft to stable tissue.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic scissors to cut a long piece of redundant graft, as robotic scissors are not sharp enough for this task.
The graft appears rolled up like a cord rather than flat, with the ureter visible on the right side, showing distinct vermiculation.
The surgeon completes the fixation of the graft, securing it in place during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to close the perineum, likely using sutures to approximate the tissue layers in the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs the closure of the peritoneum, ensuring the tissue layers are properly aligned and secured.
The rectum appears reasonably straight in the surgical field.
The surgeon unslings the uterus, revealing the final result of the rectopexy procedure.
